Environment: Enterprise core topology linking WAN-Router-01 to WAN-Router-02 on VLAN 50.
Business Context: Users at a branch office report they can browse simple web sites, but cannot download large files or open core database pages over the GRE tunnel linked to WAN-Router-01.
Problem Statement: GRE Tunnel drops large packets due to PMTUD path failure (DF bit set).
Symptoms: Web sites hang indefinitely during load. Ping with small packet size (1000 bytes) works, but ping with 1500 bytes and DF bit set fails.
Error Messages: %TUN-5-MTU_ERR: Packet dropped due to GRE encapsulation size exceeding interface MTU
Logs:
2026-06-27T12:50:01Z WAN-Router-01 %IP-4-FRAG_REQUIRED: Packet from 10.10.100.5 to 192.168.10.10 requires fragmentation but DF bit set
2026-06-27T12:50:04Z WAN-Router-01 ICMP: sending dst unreachable; frag needed to 10.10.100.5
Monitoring Alerts: GRE-Tunnel-Frag-Error: Packet drop rate on Tunnel0 exceeds threshold. Severity: Major.
Root Cause: The GRE tunnel adds a 24-byte header, reducing the payload MTU to 1476. The client PC sends 1500-byte packets with the Don't Fragment (DF) bit set. Upstream devices drop these packets without sending ICMP Type 3 Code 4 (frag needed) because ICMP was blocked by firewalls.
Step-by-Step Troubleshooting:
1. Run ping tests over the tunnel with varying packet sizes and DF bit enabled.
2. Verify TCP MSS adjustment configurations on the tunnel interfaces.
3. Configure 'ip tcp adjust-mss 1360' on the tunnel interfaces.
Commands Executed:
ping 192.168.10.10 size 1450 df-bit
show interface tunnel 0
Expected Output: Successful ping at 1450 bytes with DF bit.
Actual Output: Ping fails for packets larger than 1420 bytes.
Resolution: Configured 'ip tcp adjust-mss 1360' and 'ip mtu 1400' on the Tunnel0 interface of WAN-Router-01 and the branch router.
Validation Steps: Verify client web page loads fully and runs without packet dropouts.
Rollback Procedure:
no ip tcp adjust-mss
no ip mtu
Preventive Measures: Configure TCP MSS adjustment on all GRE/IPSec tunnels during deployment.
Best Practices: Set TCP MSS to 1360 bytes on VPN interfaces to account for IPSec and GRE encapsulation overhead.
Security Considerations: Permit ICMP Type 3 Code 4 in edge firewall rules to support Path MTU Discovery.
Automation Opportunity: Ansible playbook implementing interface MTU and MSS settings on tunnel interfaces.
AI Troubleshooting Prompt: Resolve GRE packet fragmentation drops. Set tunnel interface MTU to 1400 and TCP MSS to 1360.
Interview Questions: Why does GRE encapsulation require adjustments to the TCP Maximum Segment Size (MSS)?
References: RFC 2923 - TCP Problems with Path MTU Discovery.

Environment: Enterprise core topology linking vEdge-Branch-1 to vSmart-Control-1 on VLAN 60.
Business Context: A bandwidth upgrade on the WAN link between vEdge-Branch-1 and vSmart-Control-1 changed the physical interface from Ethernet to Fiber. The link was established, but routing tables failed to sync, stopping inter-office email access.
Problem Statement: OSPF neighbor adjacency stuck in EXSTART/EXCHANGE state due to MTU mismatch on peer interfaces.
Symptoms: OSPF adjacency fails to reach the FULL state, cycling between EXSTART and EXCHANGE. Routes are not advertised.
Error Messages: %OSPF-5-ADJCHG: Process 1, Nbr 1.1.1.1 on GigabitEthernet1/0/14 from LOADING to DOWN, Neighbor Down: Interface MTU mismatch
Logs:
2026-06-27T12:30:00Z vEdge-Branch-1 %OSPF-5-ADJCHG: Neighbor 1.1.1.1 stuck in EXSTART
2026-06-27T12:30:04Z vEdge-Branch-1 OSPF: Retransmitting DBD packet to 1.1.1.1 on GigabitEthernet1/0/14
Monitoring Alerts: OSPF-Adjacency-EXSTART: Neighbor 1.1.1.1 is stuck. Severity: Major.
Root Cause: The MTU setting on vEdge-Branch-1's interface GigabitEthernet1/0/14 is 1500 bytes, whereas the remote peer vSmart-Control-1 is configured with an MTU of 1470 bytes. OSPF Database Description (DBD) packets are dropped when they exceed the lower MTU limit.
Step-by-Step Troubleshooting:
1. Verify interface MTU settings using 'show interfaces GigabitEthernet1/0/14'.
2. Run 'show ip ospf neighbor' to confirm state.
3. Configure matching MTU values on both ends or set OSPF to ignore MTU mismatches.
Commands Executed:
show ip ospf neighbor
show interfaces GigabitEthernet1/0/14
Actual Output: Local interface MTU: 1500, Peer interface MTU: 1470
Resolution: Adjusted the MTU on vEdge-Branch-1's interface GigabitEthernet1/0/14 to 1470 bytes using 'ip mtu 1470' or enabled OSPF MTU ignore using 'ip ospf mtu-ignore'.
Validation Steps: Verify adjacency state reaches FULL by running 'show ip ospf neighbor'.
Rollback Procedure: Restore previous MTU settings: 'interface GigabitEthernet1/0/14' -> 'ip mtu 1500'
Preventive Measures: Ensure WAN interface deployment check-lists specify matching MTU values.
Best Practices: Always keep standard MTU (1500 bytes) across all core interfaces unless Jumbo Frames (9000 bytes) are required.
Security Considerations: Use OSPF MD5 authentication to protect routing adjacency changes.
Automation Opportunity: Ansible playbook implementing interface MTU validations.
AI Troubleshooting Prompt: Identify OSPF adjacency stuck in EXSTART. Align interface MTUs to match 1470 bytes.
Interview Questions: Why does OSPF fail to form adjacency if there is an MTU mismatch? What packet types are affected?
References: Cisco OSPF MTU Mismatch Troubleshooting Guide.

Environment: Enterprise core topology linking PE-Router-01 to P-Router-02 on VLAN 70.
Business Context: A security policy audit required password rotation on all external BGP connections. After updating PE-Router-01, the BGP peer connection to ISP gateway P-Router-02 dropped, breaking internet access for the branch office.
Problem Statement: BGP peering fails to establish due to MD5 authentication password mismatch.
Symptoms: BGP neighbor connection stays in Active or Idle state. No routes are exchanged.
Error Messages: %TCP-6-BADAUTH: No MD5 digest from 10.255.0.2:179 to 10.255.0.1:49152
Logs:
2026-06-27T12:35:01Z PE-Router-01 %TCP-6-BADAUTH: Invalid MD5 digest from 10.255.0.2
2026-06-27T12:35:05Z PE-Router-01 %BGP-5-ADJCHANGE: neighbor 10.255.0.2 Down: Authentication Failure
Monitoring Alerts: BGP-Neighbor-Auth-Failed: BGP session with 10.255.0.2 is down. Severity: Critical.
Root Cause: The MD5 password configured locally on PE-Router-01 was set incorrectly (missing trailing characters) compared to the password on ISP gateway P-Router-02.
Step-by-Step Troubleshooting:
1. Run 'show ip bgp neighbors' to view peering state.
2. Verify the BGP neighbor password command in the active configuration.
3. Verify the password with the ISP administrator.
Commands Executed:
show ip bgp neighbors
show run | section bgp
Actual Output: BGP Neighbor State: Active
Resolution: Corrected the neighbor password configuration on PE-Router-01 under BGP AS 66406 to match the peer password.
Validation Steps: Verify session establishes with 'show ip bgp summary'.
Rollback Procedure: Revert to original password string using Cisco commands.
Preventive Measures: Use automated password vault syncing to deploy BGP keys securely and simultaneously.
Best Practices: Rotate passwords only during scheduled maintenance windows with peer operators present.
Security Considerations: Use strong unique passwords for BGP sessions and restrict VTY access.
Automation Opportunity: Ansible task utilizing vault secrets to deploy neighbor passwords.
AI Troubleshooting Prompt: Identify BGP TCP-6-BADAUTH errors. Update the neighbor password config to match the ISP key.
Interview Questions: How does BGP utilize TCP MD5 signatures to protect peering sessions? What port does BGP run on?
References: RFC 2385 - Protection of BGP Sessions via the TCP MD5 Signature Option.

Environment: Enterprise core topology linking ASA-Firewall-01 to ASA-Firewall-02 on VLAN 80.
Business Context: An employee connected a personal desktop switch to their cubicle wall jack. Immediately, all network access to that desk was lost, prompting an urgent desktop support ticket.
Problem Statement: Switch access interface shutdown by Spanning Tree BPDU Guard after receiving BPDUs.
Symptoms: Workstation loses link state. Interface LED is solid orange/amber. Link status shows err-disable.
Error Messages: %SPANTREE-2-BLOCK_BPDUGUARD: Received BPDU on port GigabitEthernet1/0/16 with BPDU Guard enabled. Disabling port.
Logs:
2026-06-27T12:40:02Z ASA-Firewall-01 %PM-4-ERR_DISABLE: bpduguard error detected on GigabitEthernet1/0/16, putting GigabitEthernet1/0/16 in err-disable state
2026-06-27T12:40:03Z ASA-Firewall-01 %LINK-3-UPDOWN: Interface GigabitEthernet1/0/16, changed state to Down
Monitoring Alerts: Port-Err-Disabled: Interface GigabitEthernet1/0/16 shutdown by BPDU Guard. Severity: Major.
Root Cause: BPDU Guard was active on the interface configured with PortFast. Connecting a switch caused BPDU frames to be sent to ASA-Firewall-01, which triggered the security block to prevent STP loops.
Step-by-Step Troubleshooting:
1. Run 'show interface GigabitEthernet1/0/16 status' to verify err-disable reason.
2. Unplug the unauthorized switch.
3. Shut down and re-enable the port or configure errdisable recovery.
Commands Executed:
show interface GigabitEthernet1/0/16 status
show spanning-tree summary
Actual Output: Port state: err-disabled
Resolution: Disconnected the rogue switch and executed 'shutdown' / 'no shutdown' on interface GigabitEthernet1/0/16 to clear the err-disable state.
Validation Steps: Confirm client PC receives IP via DHCP and state shows connected.
Rollback Procedure: None required once rogue hardware is removed.
Preventive Measures: Keep BPDU Guard enabled globally on all access ports to block unauthorized switches.
Best Practices: Enable 'errdisable recovery cause bpduguard' to automatically restore ports after a timeout period.
Security Considerations: Use Port Security in conjunction with BPDU Guard to limit MAC address counts.
Automation Opportunity: Automated alert script to notify port location when BPDU Guard triggers.
AI Troubleshooting Prompt: Clear err-disable state on port GigabitEthernet1/0/16 after verifying the unauthorized switch has been disconnected.
Interview Questions: Why is BPDU Guard deployed on ports configured with PortFast? What is the risk if it is disabled?
References: Cisco Spanning Tree BPDU Guard Configuration Guide.

Environment: Enterprise core topology linking HQ-Core-Router-01 to Branch-Router-03 on VLAN 90.
Business Context: To increase database synchronization speeds between HQ-Core-Router-01 and Branch-Router-03, engineers bundled two interfaces into a Port-channel. However, the throughput dropped below single link speeds.
Problem Statement: LACP Port-channel bundle degraded due to port speed/duplex mismatch on member interfaces.
Symptoms: Port-channel throughput is low. One interface shows suspended. Interface packet drops increase.
Error Messages: %EC-5-CANNOT_BUNDLE2: GigabitEthernet1/0/2 is not compatible with GigabitEthernet1/0/1 and cannot be bundled
Logs:
2026-06-27T12:45:01Z HQ-Core-Router-01 %EC-5-CANNOT_BUNDLE2: Gi1/0/2 speed is 100Mb, Gi1/0/1 speed is 1Gb
2026-06-27T12:45:04Z HQ-Core-Router-01 %EC-5-PORT_SUSPENDED: Interface Gi1/0/2 suspended in Port-channel 1
Monitoring Alerts: LACP-Bundle-Degraded: Port-channel 1 has suspended members. Severity: Warning.
Root Cause: Interface GigabitEthernet1/0/2 negotiated a speed of 100Mbps (half-duplex) due to a bad patch cable, while GigabitEthernet1/0/1 negotiated 1Gbps. LACP requires all member ports to have identical speed, duplex, and VLAN settings.
Step-by-Step Troubleshooting:
1. Run 'show etherchannel summary' to find suspended ports.
2. Check physical link speeds using 'show interfaces status'.
3. Replace the bad cable or force speed settings.
Commands Executed:
show etherchannel summary
show interfaces status
Expected Output: All ports bundled (P state) and running at 1Gbps.
Actual Output: Gi1/0/2 marked as (s) suspended due to speed mismatch.
Resolution: Replaced the degraded network cable on Gi1/0/2, allowing it to negotiate 1Gbps, resolving the bundle conflict.
Validation Steps: Verify bundle status shows 'P' (in Port-channel) for all members.
Rollback Procedure: None needed.
Preventive Measures: Enforce auto-negotiation on all bundle members and test cabling profiles.
Best Practices: Ensure bundle member ports are spread across different switch linecards for hardware redundancy.
Security Considerations: Use LACP system priorities to prevent spoofed switches from hijacking the bundle.
Automation Opportunity: Python script checking port-channel member counts via SNMP.
AI Troubleshooting Prompt: Identify suspended ports in Port-channel 1. Replace degraded links and verify state.
Interview Questions: What parameters must match for physical interfaces to be successfully bundled into an LACP group?
References: IEEE 802.3ad - Link Aggregation Control Protocol.

Problem Statement: OSPF neighbor adjacency stuck in EXSTART/EXCHANGE state due to MTU mismatch on peer interfaces.
Symptoms: OSPF adjacency fails to reach the FULL state, cycling between EXSTART and EXCHANGE. Routes are not advertised.
Error Messages: %OSPF-5-ADJCHG: Process 1, Nbr 192.168.5.254 on GigabitEthernet1/0/24 from LOADING to DOWN, Neighbor Down: Interface MTU mismatch
Logs:
2026-06-27T12:30:00Z ASA-Firewall-01 %OSPF-5-ADJCHG: Neighbor 192.168.5.254 stuck in EXSTART
2026-06-27T12:30:04Z ASA-Firewall-01 OSPF: Retransmitting DBD packet to 192.168.5.254 on GigabitEthernet1/0/24
Monitoring Alerts: OSPF-Adjacency-EXSTART: Neighbor 192.168.5.254 is stuck. Severity: Major.
Root Cause: The MTU setting on ASA-Firewall-01's interface GigabitEthernet1/0/24 is 1500 bytes, whereas the remote peer ASA-Firewall-02 is configured with an MTU of 1490 bytes. OSPF Database Description (DBD) packets are dropped when they exceed the lower MTU limit.
Step-by-Step Troubleshooting:
1. Verify interface MTU settings using 'show interfaces GigabitEthernet1/0/24'.
2. Run 'show ip ospf neighbor' to confirm state.
3. Configure matching MTU values on both ends or set OSPF to ignore MTU mismatches.
Commands Executed:
show ip ospf neighbor
show interfaces GigabitEthernet1/0/24
Actual Output: Local interface MTU: 1500, Peer interface MTU: 1490
Resolution: Adjusted the MTU on ASA-Firewall-01's interface GigabitEthernet1/0/24 to 1490 bytes using 'ip mtu 1490' or enabled OSPF MTU ignore using 'ip ospf mtu-ignore'.
Validation Steps: Verify adjacency state reaches FULL by running 'show ip ospf neighbor'.
Rollback Procedure: Restore previous MTU settings: 'interface GigabitEthernet1/0/24' -> 'ip mtu 1500'
Preventive Measures: Ensure WAN interface deployment check-lists specify matching MTU values.
Best Practices: Always keep standard MTU (1500 bytes) across all core interfaces unless Jumbo Frames (9000 bytes) are required.
Security Considerations: Use OSPF MD5 authentication to protect routing adjacency changes.
Automation Opportunity: Ansible playbook implementing interface MTU validations.
AI Troubleshooting Prompt: Identify OSPF adjacency stuck in EXSTART. Align interface MTUs to match 1490 bytes.
Interview Questions: Why does OSPF fail to form adjacency if there is an MTU mismatch? What packet types are affected?
References: Cisco OSPF MTU Mismatch Troubleshooting Guide.

Environment: Enterprise core topology linking HQ-Core-Router-01 to Branch-Router-03 on VLAN 170.
Business Context: A security policy audit required password rotation on all external BGP connections. After updating HQ-Core-Router-01, the BGP peer connection to ISP gateway Branch-Router-03 dropped, breaking internet access for the branch office.
Problem Statement: BGP peering fails to establish due to MD5 authentication password mismatch.
Symptoms: BGP neighbor connection stays in Active or Idle state. No routes are exchanged.
Logs:
2026-06-27T12:35:01Z HQ-Core-Router-01 %TCP-6-BADAUTH: Invalid MD5 digest from 192.168.20.1
2026-06-27T12:35:05Z HQ-Core-Router-01 %BGP-5-ADJCHANGE: neighbor 192.168.20.1 Down: Authentication Failure
Monitoring Alerts: BGP-Neighbor-Auth-Failed: BGP session with 192.168.20.1 is down. Severity: Critical.
Root Cause: The MD5 password configured locally on HQ-Core-Router-01 was set incorrectly (missing trailing characters) compared to the password on ISP gateway Branch-Router-03.
Step-by-Step Troubleshooting:
1. Run 'show ip bgp neighbors' to view peering state.
2. Verify the BGP neighbor password command in the active configuration.
3. Verify the password with the ISP administrator.
Commands Executed:
show ip bgp neighbors
show run | section bgp
Actual Output: BGP Neighbor State: Active
Resolution: Corrected the neighbor password configuration on HQ-Core-Router-01 under BGP AS 66416 to match the peer password.
Validation Steps: Verify session establishes with 'show ip bgp summary'.
Rollback Procedure: Revert to original password string using Cisco commands.
Preventive Measures: Use automated password vault syncing to deploy BGP keys securely and simultaneously.
Best Practices: Rotate passwords only during scheduled maintenance windows with peer operators present.
Security Considerations: Use strong unique passwords for BGP sessions and restrict VTY access.
Automation Opportunity: Ansible task utilizing vault secrets to deploy neighbor passwords.
AI Troubleshooting Prompt: Identify BGP TCP-6-BADAUTH errors. Update the neighbor password config to match the ISP key.
Interview Questions: How does BGP utilize TCP MD5 signatures to protect peering sessions? What port does BGP run on?
References: RFC 2385 - Protection of BGP Sessions via the TCP MD5 Signature Option.

Environment: Enterprise core topology linking DC-Gateway-01 to Partner-Router-02 on VLAN 180.
Business Context: An employee connected a personal desktop switch to their cubicle wall jack. Immediately, all network access to that desk was lost, prompting an urgent desktop support ticket.
Problem Statement: Switch access interface shutdown by Spanning Tree BPDU Guard after receiving BPDUs.
Symptoms: Workstation loses link state. Interface LED is solid orange/amber. Link status shows err-disable.
Error Messages: %SPANTREE-2-BLOCK_BPDUGUARD: Received BPDU on port GigabitEthernet1/0/2 with BPDU Guard enabled. Disabling port.
Logs:
2026-06-27T12:40:02Z DC-Gateway-01 %PM-4-ERR_DISABLE: bpduguard error detected on GigabitEthernet1/0/2, putting GigabitEthernet1/0/2 in err-disable state
2026-06-27T12:40:03Z DC-Gateway-01 %LINK-3-UPDOWN: Interface GigabitEthernet1/0/2, changed state to Down
Monitoring Alerts: Port-Err-Disabled: Interface GigabitEthernet1/0/2 shutdown by BPDU Guard. Severity: Major.
Root Cause: BPDU Guard was active on the interface configured with PortFast. Connecting a switch caused BPDU frames to be sent to DC-Gateway-01, which triggered the security block to prevent STP loops.
Step-by-Step Troubleshooting:
1. Run 'show interface GigabitEthernet1/0/2 status' to verify err-disable reason.
2. Unplug the unauthorized switch.
3. Shut down and re-enable the port or configure errdisable recovery.
Commands Executed:
show interface GigabitEthernet1/0/2 status
show spanning-tree summary
Actual Output: Port state: err-disabled
Resolution: Disconnected the rogue switch and executed 'shutdown' / 'no shutdown' on interface GigabitEthernet1/0/2 to clear the err-disable state.
Validation Steps: Confirm client PC receives IP via DHCP and state shows connected.
Rollback Procedure: None required once rogue hardware is removed.
Preventive Measures: Keep BPDU Guard enabled globally on all access ports to block unauthorized switches.
Best Practices: Enable 'errdisable recovery cause bpduguard' to automatically restore ports after a timeout period.
Security Considerations: Use Port Security in conjunction with BPDU Guard to limit MAC address counts.
Automation Opportunity: Automated alert script to notify port location when BPDU Guard triggers.
AI Troubleshooting Prompt: Clear err-disable state on port GigabitEthernet1/0/2 after verifying the unauthorized switch has been disconnected.
Interview Questions: Why is BPDU Guard deployed on ports configured with PortFast? What is the risk if it is disabled?
References: Cisco Spanning Tree BPDU Guard Configuration Guide.

Environment: Enterprise core topology linking HQ-Edge-01 to ISP-Gateway-01 on VLAN 190.
Business Context: To increase database synchronization speeds between HQ-Edge-01 and ISP-Gateway-01, engineers bundled two interfaces into a Port-channel. However, the throughput dropped below single link speeds.
Problem Statement: LACP Port-channel bundle degraded due to port speed/duplex mismatch on member interfaces.
Symptoms: Port-channel throughput is low. One interface shows suspended. Interface packet drops increase.
Error Messages: %EC-5-CANNOT_BUNDLE2: GigabitEthernet1/0/2 is not compatible with GigabitEthernet1/0/1 and cannot be bundled
Logs:
2026-06-27T12:45:01Z HQ-Edge-01 %EC-5-CANNOT_BUNDLE2: Gi1/0/2 speed is 100Mb, Gi1/0/1 speed is 1Gb
2026-06-27T12:45:04Z HQ-Edge-01 %EC-5-PORT_SUSPENDED: Interface Gi1/0/2 suspended in Port-channel 3
Monitoring Alerts: LACP-Bundle-Degraded: Port-channel 3 has suspended members. Severity: Warning.
Root Cause: Interface GigabitEthernet1/0/2 negotiated a speed of 100Mbps (half-duplex) due to a bad patch cable, while GigabitEthernet1/0/1 negotiated 1Gbps. LACP requires all member ports to have identical speed, duplex, and VLAN settings.
Step-by-Step Troubleshooting:
1. Run 'show etherchannel summary' to find suspended ports.
2. Check physical link speeds using 'show interfaces status'.
3. Replace the bad cable or force speed settings.
Commands Executed:
show etherchannel summary
show interfaces status
Expected Output: All ports bundled (P state) and running at 1Gbps.
Actual Output: Gi1/0/2 marked as (s) suspended due to speed mismatch.
Resolution: Replaced the degraded network cable on Gi1/0/2, allowing it to negotiate 1Gbps, resolving the bundle conflict.
Validation Steps: Verify bundle status shows 'P' (in Port-channel) for all members.
Rollback Procedure: None needed.
Preventive Measures: Enforce auto-negotiation on all bundle members and test cabling profiles.
Best Practices: Ensure bundle member ports are spread across different switch linecards for hardware redundancy.
Security Considerations: Use LACP system priorities to prevent spoofed switches from hijacking the bundle.
Automation Opportunity: Python script checking port-channel member counts via SNMP.
AI Troubleshooting Prompt: Identify suspended ports in Port-channel 3. Replace degraded links and verify state.
Interview Questions: What parameters must match for physical interfaces to be successfully bundled into an LACP group?
References: IEEE 802.3ad - Link Aggregation Control Protocol.

Environment: Enterprise core topology linking SW-Core-A to SW-Distribution-B on VLAN 200.
Business Context: Users at a branch office report they can browse simple web sites, but cannot download large files or open core database pages over the GRE tunnel linked to SW-Core-A.
Problem Statement: GRE Tunnel drops large packets due to PMTUD path failure (DF bit set).
Symptoms: Web sites hang indefinitely during load. Ping with small packet size (1000 bytes) works, but ping with 1500 bytes and DF bit set fails.
Error Messages: %TUN-5-MTU_ERR: Packet dropped due to GRE encapsulation size exceeding interface MTU
Logs:
2026-06-27T12:50:01Z SW-Core-A %IP-4-FRAG_REQUIRED: Packet from 10.10.100.5 to 192.168.10.10 requires fragmentation but DF bit set
2026-06-27T12:50:04Z SW-Core-A ICMP: sending dst unreachable; frag needed to 10.10.100.5
Monitoring Alerts: GRE-Tunnel-Frag-Error: Packet drop rate on Tunnel0 exceeds threshold. Severity: Major.
Root Cause: The GRE tunnel adds a 24-byte header, reducing the payload MTU to 1476. The client PC sends 1500-byte packets with the Don't Fragment (DF) bit set. Upstream devices drop these packets without sending ICMP Type 3 Code 4 (frag needed) because ICMP was blocked by firewalls.
Step-by-Step Troubleshooting:
1. Run ping tests over the tunnel with varying packet sizes and DF bit enabled.
2. Verify TCP MSS adjustment configurations on the tunnel interfaces.
3. Configure 'ip tcp adjust-mss 1360' on the tunnel interfaces.
Commands Executed:
ping 192.168.10.10 size 1450 df-bit
show interface tunnel 0
Expected Output: Successful ping at 1450 bytes with DF bit.
Actual Output: Ping fails for packets larger than 1420 bytes.
Resolution: Configured 'ip tcp adjust-mss 1360' and 'ip mtu 1400' on the Tunnel0 interface of SW-Core-A and the branch router.
Validation Steps: Verify client web page loads fully and runs without packet dropouts.
Rollback Procedure:
no ip tcp adjust-mss
no ip mtu
Preventive Measures: Configure TCP MSS adjustment on all GRE/IPSec tunnels during deployment.
Best Practices: Set TCP MSS to 1360 bytes on VPN interfaces to account for IPSec and GRE encapsulation overhead.
Security Considerations: Permit ICMP Type 3 Code 4 in edge firewall rules to support Path MTU Discovery.
Automation Opportunity: Ansible playbook implementing interface MTU and MSS settings on tunnel interfaces.
AI Troubleshooting Prompt: Resolve GRE packet fragmentation drops. Set tunnel interface MTU to 1400 and TCP MSS to 1360.
Interview Questions: Why does GRE encapsulation require adjustments to the TCP Maximum Segment Size (MSS)?
References: RFC 2923 - TCP Problems with Path MTU Discovery.

2026-06-27T12:35:05Z HQ-Edge-01 %BGP-5-ADJCHANGE: neighbor 203.0.113.2 Down: Authentication Failure
Monitoring Alerts: BGP-Neighbor-Auth-Failed: BGP session with 203.0.113.2 is down. Severity: Critical.
Root Cause: The MD5 password configured locally on HQ-Edge-01 was set incorrectly (missing trailing characters) compared to the password on ISP gateway ISP-Gateway-01.
Step-by-Step Troubleshooting:
1. Run 'show ip bgp neighbors' to view peering state.
2. Verify the BGP neighbor password command in the active configuration.
3. Verify the password with the ISP administrator.
Commands Executed:
show ip bgp neighbors
show run | section bgp

Resolution: Corrected the neighbor password configuration on HQ-Edge-01 under BGP AS 66426 to match the peer password.
Validation Steps: Verify session establishes with 'show ip bgp summary'.
Rollback Procedure: Revert to original password string using Cisco commands.
Preventive Measures: Use automated password vault syncing to deploy BGP keys securely and simultaneously.
Best Practices: Rotate passwords only during scheduled maintenance windows with peer operators present.

Environment: Enterprise core topology linking SW-Core-A to SW-Distribution-B on VLAN 80.
Business Context: An employee connected a personal desktop switch to their cubicle wall jack. Immediately, all network access to that desk was lost, prompting an urgent desktop support ticket.
Problem Statement: Switch access interface shutdown by Spanning Tree BPDU Guard after receiving BPDUs.
Symptoms: Workstation loses link state. Interface LED is solid orange/amber. Link status shows err-disable.
Error Messages: %SPANTREE-2-BLOCK_BPDUGUARD: Received BPDU on port GigabitEthernet1/0/12 with BPDU Guard enabled. Disabling port.
Logs:
2026-06-27T12:40:02Z SW-Core-A %PM-4-ERR_DISABLE: bpduguard error detected on GigabitEthernet1/0/12, putting GigabitEthernet1/0/12 in err-disable state
2026-06-27T12:40:03Z SW-Core-A %LINK-3-UPDOWN: Interface GigabitEthernet1/0/12, changed state to Down
Monitoring Alerts: Port-Err-Disabled: Interface GigabitEthernet1/0/12 shutdown by BPDU Guard. Severity: Major.
Root Cause: BPDU Guard was active on the interface configured with PortFast. Connecting a switch caused BPDU frames to be sent to SW-Core-A, which triggered the security block to prevent STP loops.
Step-by-Step Troubleshooting:
1. Run 'show interface GigabitEthernet1/0/12 status' to verify err-disable reason.
2. Unplug the unauthorized switch.
3. Shut down and re-enable the port or configure errdisable recovery.
Commands Executed:
show interface GigabitEthernet1/0/12 status
show spanning-tree summary
Actual Output: Port state: err-disabled
Resolution: Disconnected the rogue switch and executed 'shutdown' / 'no shutdown' on interface GigabitEthernet1/0/12 to clear the err-disable state.
Validation Steps: Confirm client PC receives IP via DHCP and state shows connected.
Rollback Procedure: None required once rogue hardware is removed.
Preventive Measures: Keep BPDU Guard enabled globally on all access ports to block unauthorized switches.
Best Practices: Enable 'errdisable recovery cause bpduguard' to automatically restore ports after a timeout period.
Security Considerations: Use Port Security in conjunction with BPDU Guard to limit MAC address counts.
Automation Opportunity: Automated alert script to notify port location when BPDU Guard triggers.
AI Troubleshooting Prompt: Clear err-disable state on port GigabitEthernet1/0/12 after verifying the unauthorized switch has been disconnected.
Interview Questions: Why is BPDU Guard deployed on ports configured with PortFast? What is the risk if it is disabled?
References: Cisco Spanning Tree BPDU Guard Configuration Guide.

Environment: Enterprise core topology linking WAN-Router-01 to WAN-Router-02 on VLAN 90.
Business Context: To increase database synchronization speeds between WAN-Router-01 and WAN-Router-02, engineers bundled two interfaces into a Port-channel. However, the throughput dropped below single link speeds.
Problem Statement: LACP Port-channel bundle degraded due to port speed/duplex mismatch on member interfaces.
Symptoms: Port-channel throughput is low. One interface shows suspended. Interface packet drops increase.
Error Messages: %EC-5-CANNOT_BUNDLE2: GigabitEthernet1/0/2 is not compatible with GigabitEthernet1/0/1 and cannot be bundled
Logs:
2026-06-27T12:45:01Z WAN-Router-01 %EC-5-CANNOT_BUNDLE2: Gi1/0/2 speed is 100Mb, Gi1/0/1 speed is 1Gb
2026-06-27T12:45:04Z WAN-Router-01 %EC-5-PORT_SUSPENDED: Interface Gi1/0/2 suspended in Port-channel 5
Monitoring Alerts: LACP-Bundle-Degraded: Port-channel 5 has suspended members. Severity: Warning.
Root Cause: Interface GigabitEthernet1/0/2 negotiated a speed of 100Mbps (half-duplex) due to a bad patch cable, while GigabitEthernet1/0/1 negotiated 1Gbps. LACP requires all member ports to have identical speed, duplex, and VLAN settings.
Step-by-Step Troubleshooting:
1. Run 'show etherchannel summary' to find suspended ports.
2. Check physical link speeds using 'show interfaces status'.
3. Replace the bad cable or force speed settings.
Commands Executed:
show etherchannel summary
show interfaces status
Expected Output: All ports bundled (P state) and running at 1Gbps.
Actual Output: Gi1/0/2 marked as (s) suspended due to speed mismatch.
Resolution: Replaced the degraded network cable on Gi1/0/2, allowing it to negotiate 1Gbps, resolving the bundle conflict.
Validation Steps: Verify bundle status shows 'P' (in Port-channel) for all members.
Rollback Procedure: None needed.
Preventive Measures: Enforce auto-negotiation on all bundle members and test cabling profiles.
Best Practices: Ensure bundle member ports are spread across different switch linecards for hardware redundancy.
Security Considerations: Use LACP system priorities to prevent spoofed switches from hijacking the bundle.
Automation Opportunity: Python script checking port-channel member counts via SNMP.
AI Troubleshooting Prompt: Identify suspended ports in Port-channel 5. Replace degraded links and verify state.
Interview Questions: What parameters must match for physical interfaces to be successfully bundled into an LACP group?
References: IEEE 802.3ad - Link Aggregation Control Protocol.

Environment: Enterprise core topology linking vEdge-Branch-1 to vSmart-Control-1 on VLAN 100.
Business Context: Users at a branch office report they can browse simple web sites, but cannot download large files or open core database pages over the GRE tunnel linked to vEdge-Branch-1.
Problem Statement: GRE Tunnel drops large packets due to PMTUD path failure (DF bit set).
Symptoms: Web sites hang indefinitely during load. Ping with small packet size (1000 bytes) works, but ping with 1500 bytes and DF bit set fails.
Error Messages: %TUN-5-MTU_ERR: Packet dropped due to GRE encapsulation size exceeding interface MTU
Logs:
2026-06-27T12:50:01Z vEdge-Branch-1 %IP-4-FRAG_REQUIRED: Packet from 10.10.100.5 to 192.168.10.10 requires fragmentation but DF bit set
2026-06-27T12:50:04Z vEdge-Branch-1 ICMP: sending dst unreachable; frag needed to 10.10.100.5
Monitoring Alerts: GRE-Tunnel-Frag-Error: Packet drop rate on Tunnel0 exceeds threshold. Severity: Major.
Root Cause: The GRE tunnel adds a 24-byte header, reducing the payload MTU to 1476. The client PC sends 1500-byte packets with the Don't Fragment (DF) bit set. Upstream devices drop these packets without sending ICMP Type 3 Code 4 (frag needed) because ICMP was blocked by firewalls.
Step-by-Step Troubleshooting:
1. Run ping tests over the tunnel with varying packet sizes and DF bit enabled.
2. Verify TCP MSS adjustment configurations on the tunnel interfaces.
3. Configure 'ip tcp adjust-mss 1360' on the tunnel interfaces.
Commands Executed:
ping 192.168.10.10 size 1450 df-bit
show interface tunnel 0
Expected Output: Successful ping at 1450 bytes with DF bit.
Actual Output: Ping fails for packets larger than 1420 bytes.
Resolution: Configured 'ip tcp adjust-mss 1360' and 'ip mtu 1400' on the Tunnel0 interface of vEdge-Branch-1 and the branch router.
Validation Steps: Verify client web page loads fully and runs without packet dropouts.
Rollback Procedure:
no ip tcp adjust-mss
no ip mtu
Preventive Measures: Configure TCP MSS adjustment on all GRE/IPSec tunnels during deployment.
Best Practices: Set TCP MSS to 1360 bytes on VPN interfaces to account for IPSec and GRE encapsulation overhead.
Security Considerations: Permit ICMP Type 3 Code 4 in edge firewall rules to support Path MTU Discovery.
Automation Opportunity: Ansible playbook implementing interface MTU and MSS settings on tunnel interfaces.
AI Troubleshooting Prompt: Resolve GRE packet fragmentation drops. Set tunnel interface MTU to 1400 and TCP MSS to 1360.
Interview Questions: Why does GRE encapsulation require adjustments to the TCP Maximum Segment Size (MSS)?
References: RFC 2923 - TCP Problems with Path MTU Discovery.

Environment: Enterprise core topology linking PE-Router-01 to P-Router-02 on VLAN 110.
Business Context: A bandwidth upgrade on the WAN link between PE-Router-01 and P-Router-02 changed the physical interface from Ethernet to Fiber. The link was established, but routing tables failed to sync, stopping inter-office email access.
Problem Statement: OSPF neighbor adjacency stuck in EXSTART/EXCHANGE state due to MTU mismatch on peer interfaces.
Symptoms: OSPF adjacency fails to reach the FULL state, cycling between EXSTART and EXCHANGE. Routes are not advertised.
Error Messages: %OSPF-5-ADJCHG: Process 1, Nbr 10.255.255.1 on GigabitEthernet1/0/15 from LOADING to DOWN, Neighbor Down: Interface MTU mismatch
Logs:
2026-06-27T12:30:00Z PE-Router-01 %OSPF-5-ADJCHG: Neighbor 10.255.255.1 stuck in EXSTART
2026-06-27T12:30:04Z PE-Router-01 OSPF: Retransmitting DBD packet to 10.255.255.1 on GigabitEthernet1/0/15
Monitoring Alerts: OSPF-Adjacency-EXSTART: Neighbor 10.255.255.1 is stuck. Severity: Major.
Root Cause: The MTU setting on PE-Router-01's interface GigabitEthernet1/0/15 is 1500 bytes, whereas the remote peer P-Router-02 is configured with an MTU of 1490 bytes. OSPF Database Description (DBD) packets are dropped when they exceed the lower MTU limit.
Step-by-Step Troubleshooting:
1. Verify interface MTU settings using 'show interfaces GigabitEthernet1/0/15'.
2. Run 'show ip ospf neighbor' to confirm state.
3. Configure matching MTU values on both ends or set OSPF to ignore MTU mismatches.
Commands Executed:
show ip ospf neighbor
show interfaces GigabitEthernet1/0/15
Actual Output: Local interface MTU: 1500, Peer interface MTU: 1490
Resolution: Adjusted the MTU on PE-Router-01's interface GigabitEthernet1/0/15 to 1490 bytes using 'ip mtu 1490' or enabled OSPF MTU ignore using 'ip ospf mtu-ignore'.
Validation Steps: Verify adjacency state reaches FULL by running 'show ip ospf neighbor'.
Rollback Procedure: Restore previous MTU settings: 'interface GigabitEthernet1/0/15' -> 'ip mtu 1500'
Preventive Measures: Ensure WAN interface deployment check-lists specify matching MTU values.
Best Practices: Always keep standard MTU (1500 bytes) across all core interfaces unless Jumbo Frames (9000 bytes) are required.
Security Considerations: Use OSPF MD5 authentication to protect routing adjacency changes.
Automation Opportunity: Ansible playbook implementing interface MTU validations.
AI Troubleshooting Prompt: Identify OSPF adjacency stuck in EXSTART. Align interface MTUs to match 1490 bytes.
Interview Questions: Why does OSPF fail to form adjacency if there is an MTU mismatch? What packet types are affected?
References: Cisco OSPF MTU Mismatch Troubleshooting Guide.

Environment: Enterprise core topology linking PE-Router-01 to P-Router-02 on VLAN 190.
Business Context: To increase database synchronization speeds between PE-Router-01 and P-Router-02, engineers bundled two interfaces into a Port-channel. However, the throughput dropped below single link speeds.
Problem Statement: LACP Port-channel bundle degraded due to port speed/duplex mismatch on member interfaces.
Symptoms: Port-channel throughput is low. One interface shows suspended. Interface packet drops increase.
Error Messages: %EC-5-CANNOT_BUNDLE2: GigabitEthernet1/0/2 is not compatible with GigabitEthernet1/0/1 and cannot be bundled
Logs:
2026-06-27T12:45:01Z PE-Router-01 %EC-5-CANNOT_BUNDLE2: Gi1/0/2 speed is 100Mb, Gi1/0/1 speed is 1Gb
2026-06-27T12:45:04Z PE-Router-01 %EC-5-PORT_SUSPENDED: Interface Gi1/0/2 suspended in Port-channel 7
Monitoring Alerts: LACP-Bundle-Degraded: Port-channel 7 has suspended members. Severity: Warning.
Root Cause: Interface GigabitEthernet1/0/2 negotiated a speed of 100Mbps (half-duplex) due to a bad patch cable, while GigabitEthernet1/0/1 negotiated 1Gbps. LACP requires all member ports to have identical speed, duplex, and VLAN settings.
Step-by-Step Troubleshooting:
1. Run 'show etherchannel summary' to find suspended ports.
2. Check physical link speeds using 'show interfaces status'.
3. Replace the bad cable or force speed settings.
Commands Executed:
show etherchannel summary
show interfaces status
Expected Output: All ports bundled (P state) and running at 1Gbps.
Actual Output: Gi1/0/2 marked as (s) suspended due to speed mismatch.
Resolution: Replaced the degraded network cable on Gi1/0/2, allowing it to negotiate 1Gbps, resolving the bundle conflict.
Validation Steps: Verify bundle status shows 'P' (in Port-channel) for all members.
Rollback Procedure: None needed.
Preventive Measures: Enforce auto-negotiation on all bundle members and test cabling profiles.
Best Practices: Ensure bundle member ports are spread across different switch linecards for hardware redundancy.
Security Considerations: Use LACP system priorities to prevent spoofed switches from hijacking the bundle.
Automation Opportunity: Python script checking port-channel member counts via SNMP.
AI Troubleshooting Prompt: Identify suspended ports in Port-channel 7. Replace degraded links and verify state.
Interview Questions: What parameters must match for physical interfaces to be successfully bundled into an LACP group?
References: IEEE 802.3ad - Link Aggregation Control Protocol.

Environment: Enterprise core topology linking ASA-Firewall-01 to ASA-Firewall-02 on VLAN 200.
Business Context: Users at a branch office report they can browse simple web sites, but cannot download large files or open core database pages over the GRE tunnel linked to ASA-Firewall-01.
Problem Statement: GRE Tunnel drops large packets due to PMTUD path failure (DF bit set).
Symptoms: Web sites hang indefinitely during load. Ping with small packet size (1000 bytes) works, but ping with 1500 bytes and DF bit set fails.
Error Messages: %TUN-5-MTU_ERR: Packet dropped due to GRE encapsulation size exceeding interface MTU
Logs:
2026-06-27T12:50:01Z ASA-Firewall-01 %IP-4-FRAG_REQUIRED: Packet from 10.10.100.5 to 192.168.10.10 requires fragmentation but DF bit set
2026-06-27T12:50:04Z ASA-Firewall-01 ICMP: sending dst unreachable; frag needed to 10.10.100.5
Monitoring Alerts: GRE-Tunnel-Frag-Error: Packet drop rate on Tunnel0 exceeds threshold. Severity: Major.
Root Cause: The GRE tunnel adds a 24-byte header, reducing the payload MTU to 1476. The client PC sends 1500-byte packets with the Don't Fragment (DF) bit set. Upstream devices drop these packets without sending ICMP Type 3 Code 4 (frag needed) because ICMP was blocked by firewalls.
Step-by-Step Troubleshooting:
1. Run ping tests over the tunnel with varying packet sizes and DF bit enabled.
2. Verify TCP MSS adjustment configurations on the tunnel interfaces.
3. Configure 'ip tcp adjust-mss 1360' on the tunnel interfaces.
Commands Executed:
ping 192.168.10.10 size 1450 df-bit
show interface tunnel 0
Expected Output: Successful ping at 1450 bytes with DF bit.
Actual Output: Ping fails for packets larger than 1420 bytes.
Resolution: Configured 'ip tcp adjust-mss 1360' and 'ip mtu 1400' on the Tunnel0 interface of ASA-Firewall-01 and the branch router.
Validation Steps: Verify client web page loads fully and runs without packet dropouts.
Rollback Procedure:
no ip tcp adjust-mss
no ip mtu
Preventive Measures: Configure TCP MSS adjustment on all GRE/IPSec tunnels during deployment.
Best Practices: Set TCP MSS to 1360 bytes on VPN interfaces to account for IPSec and GRE encapsulation overhead.
Security Considerations: Permit ICMP Type 3 Code 4 in edge firewall rules to support Path MTU Discovery.
Automation Opportunity: Ansible playbook implementing interface MTU and MSS settings on tunnel interfaces.
AI Troubleshooting Prompt: Resolve GRE packet fragmentation drops. Set tunnel interface MTU to 1400 and TCP MSS to 1360.
Interview Questions: Why does GRE encapsulation require adjustments to the TCP Maximum Segment Size (MSS)?
References: RFC 2923 - TCP Problems with Path MTU Discovery.

Environment: Enterprise core topology linking HQ-Core-Router-01 to Branch-Router-03 on VLAN 10.
Business Context: A bandwidth upgrade on the WAN link between HQ-Core-Router-01 and Branch-Router-03 changed the physical interface from Ethernet to Fiber. The link was established, but routing tables failed to sync, stopping inter-office email access.
Problem Statement: OSPF neighbor adjacency stuck in EXSTART/EXCHANGE state due to MTU mismatch on peer interfaces.
Symptoms: OSPF adjacency fails to reach the FULL state, cycling between EXSTART and EXCHANGE. Routes are not advertised.
Error Messages: %OSPF-5-ADJCHG: Process 1, Nbr 10.0.1.1 on GigabitEthernet1/0/1 from LOADING to DOWN, Neighbor Down: Interface MTU mismatch
Logs:
2026-06-27T12:30:00Z HQ-Core-Router-01 %OSPF-5-ADJCHG: Neighbor 10.0.1.1 stuck in EXSTART
2026-06-27T12:30:04Z HQ-Core-Router-01 OSPF: Retransmitting DBD packet to 10.0.1.1 on GigabitEthernet1/0/1
Monitoring Alerts: OSPF-Adjacency-EXSTART: Neighbor 10.0.1.1 is stuck. Severity: Major.
Root Cause: The MTU setting on HQ-Core-Router-01's interface GigabitEthernet1/0/1 is 1500 bytes, whereas the remote peer Branch-Router-03 is configured with an MTU of 1450 bytes. OSPF Database Description (DBD) packets are dropped when they exceed the lower MTU limit.
Step-by-Step Troubleshooting:
1. Verify interface MTU settings using 'show interfaces GigabitEthernet1/0/1'.
2. Run 'show ip ospf neighbor' to confirm state.
3. Configure matching MTU values on both ends or set OSPF to ignore MTU mismatches.
Commands Executed:
show ip ospf neighbor
show interfaces GigabitEthernet1/0/1
Actual Output: Local interface MTU: 1500, Peer interface MTU: 1450
Resolution: Adjusted the MTU on HQ-Core-Router-01's interface GigabitEthernet1/0/1 to 1450 bytes using 'ip mtu 1450' or enabled OSPF MTU ignore using 'ip ospf mtu-ignore'.
Validation Steps: Verify adjacency state reaches FULL by running 'show ip ospf neighbor'.
Rollback Procedure: Restore previous MTU settings: 'interface GigabitEthernet1/0/1' -> 'ip mtu 1500'
Preventive Measures: Ensure WAN interface deployment check-lists specify matching MTU values.
Best Practices: Always keep standard MTU (1500 bytes) across all core interfaces unless Jumbo Frames (9000 bytes) are required.
Security Considerations: Use OSPF MD5 authentication to protect routing adjacency changes.
Automation Opportunity: Ansible playbook implementing interface MTU validations.
AI Troubleshooting Prompt: Identify OSPF adjacency stuck in EXSTART. Align interface MTUs to match 1450 bytes.
Interview Questions: Why does OSPF fail to form adjacency if there is an MTU mismatch? What packet types are affected?
References: Cisco OSPF MTU Mismatch Troubleshooting Guide.

Environment: Enterprise core topology linking DC-Gateway-01 to Partner-Router-02 on VLAN 20.
Business Context: A security policy audit required password rotation on all external BGP connections. After updating DC-Gateway-01, the BGP peer connection to ISP gateway Partner-Router-02 dropped, breaking internet access for the branch office.
Problem Statement: BGP peering fails to establish due to MD5 authentication password mismatch.
Symptoms: BGP neighbor connection stays in Active or Idle state. No routes are exchanged.
Error Messages: %TCP-6-BADAUTH: No MD5 digest from 172.16.50.2:179 to 172.16.50.1:49152
Logs:
2026-06-27T12:35:01Z DC-Gateway-01 %TCP-6-BADAUTH: Invalid MD5 digest from 172.16.50.2
2026-06-27T12:35:05Z DC-Gateway-01 %BGP-5-ADJCHANGE: neighbor 172.16.50.2 Down: Authentication Failure
Monitoring Alerts: BGP-Neighbor-Auth-Failed: BGP session with 172.16.50.2 is down. Severity: Critical.
Root Cause: The MD5 password configured locally on DC-Gateway-01 was set incorrectly (missing trailing characters) compared to the password on ISP gateway Partner-Router-02.
Step-by-Step Troubleshooting:
1. Run 'show ip bgp neighbors' to view peering state.
2. Verify the BGP neighbor password command in the active configuration.
3. Verify the password with the ISP administrator.
Commands Executed:
show ip bgp neighbors
show run | section bgp
Actual Output: BGP Neighbor State: Active
Resolution: Corrected the neighbor password configuration on DC-Gateway-01 under BGP AS 66441 to match the peer password.
Validation Steps: Verify session establishes with 'show ip bgp summary'.
Rollback Procedure: Revert to original password string using Cisco commands.
Preventive Measures: Use automated password vault syncing to deploy BGP keys securely and simultaneously.
Best Practices: Rotate passwords only during scheduled maintenance windows with peer operators present.
Security Considerations: Use strong unique passwords for BGP sessions and restrict VTY access.
Automation Opportunity: Ansible task utilizing vault secrets to deploy neighbor passwords.
AI Troubleshooting Prompt: Identify BGP TCP-6-BADAUTH errors. Update the neighbor password config to match the ISP key.
Interview Questions: How does BGP utilize TCP MD5 signatures to protect peering sessions? What port does BGP run on?
References: RFC 2385 - Protection of BGP Sessions via the TCP MD5 Signature Option.

Environment: Enterprise core topology linking DC-Gateway-01 to Partner-Router-02 on VLAN 100.
Business Context: Users at a branch office report they can browse simple web sites, but cannot download large files or open core database pages over the GRE tunnel linked to DC-Gateway-01.
Problem Statement: GRE Tunnel drops large packets due to PMTUD path failure (DF bit set).
Symptoms: Web sites hang indefinitely during load. Ping with small packet size (1000 bytes) works, but ping with 1500 bytes and DF bit set fails.
Error Messages: %TUN-5-MTU_ERR: Packet dropped due to GRE encapsulation size exceeding interface MTU
Logs:
2026-06-27T12:50:01Z DC-Gateway-01 %IP-4-FRAG_REQUIRED: Packet from 10.10.100.5 to 192.168.10.10 requires fragmentation but DF bit set
2026-06-27T12:50:04Z DC-Gateway-01 ICMP: sending dst unreachable; frag needed to 10.10.100.5
Monitoring Alerts: GRE-Tunnel-Frag-Error: Packet drop rate on Tunnel0 exceeds threshold. Severity: Major.
Root Cause: The GRE tunnel adds a 24-byte header, reducing the payload MTU to 1476. The client PC sends 1500-byte packets with the Don't Fragment (DF) bit set. Upstream devices drop these packets without sending ICMP Type 3 Code 4 (frag needed) because ICMP was blocked by firewalls.
Step-by-Step Troubleshooting:
1. Run ping tests over the tunnel with varying packet sizes and DF bit enabled.
2. Verify TCP MSS adjustment configurations on the tunnel interfaces.
3. Configure 'ip tcp adjust-mss 1360' on the tunnel interfaces.
Commands Executed:
ping 192.168.10.10 size 1450 df-bit
show interface tunnel 0
Expected Output: Successful ping at 1450 bytes with DF bit.
Actual Output: Ping fails for packets larger than 1420 bytes.
Resolution: Configured 'ip tcp adjust-mss 1360' and 'ip mtu 1400' on the Tunnel0 interface of DC-Gateway-01 and the branch router.
Validation Steps: Verify client web page loads fully and runs without packet dropouts.
Rollback Procedure:
no ip tcp adjust-mss
no ip mtu
Preventive Measures: Configure TCP MSS adjustment on all GRE/IPSec tunnels during deployment.
Best Practices: Set TCP MSS to 1360 bytes on VPN interfaces to account for IPSec and GRE encapsulation overhead.
Security Considerations: Permit ICMP Type 3 Code 4 in edge firewall rules to support Path MTU Discovery.
Automation Opportunity: Ansible playbook implementing interface MTU and MSS settings on tunnel interfaces.
AI Troubleshooting Prompt: Resolve GRE packet fragmentation drops. Set tunnel interface MTU to 1400 and TCP MSS to 1360.
Interview Questions: Why does GRE encapsulation require adjustments to the TCP Maximum Segment Size (MSS)?
References: RFC 2923 - TCP Problems with Path MTU Discovery.

Environment: Enterprise core topology linking HQ-Edge-01 to ISP-Gateway-01 on VLAN 110.
Business Context: A bandwidth upgrade on the WAN link between HQ-Edge-01 and ISP-Gateway-01 changed the physical interface from Ethernet to Fiber. The link was established, but routing tables failed to sync, stopping inter-office email access.
Problem Statement: OSPF neighbor adjacency stuck in EXSTART/EXCHANGE state due to MTU mismatch on peer interfaces.
Symptoms: OSPF adjacency fails to reach the FULL state, cycling between EXSTART and EXCHANGE. Routes are not advertised.
Error Messages: %OSPF-5-ADJCHG: Process 1, Nbr 8.8.8.8 on GigabitEthernet1/0/11 from LOADING to DOWN, Neighbor Down: Interface MTU mismatch
Logs:
2026-06-27T12:30:00Z HQ-Edge-01 %OSPF-5-ADJCHG: Neighbor 8.8.8.8 stuck in EXSTART
2026-06-27T12:30:04Z HQ-Edge-01 OSPF: Retransmitting DBD packet to 8.8.8.8 on GigabitEthernet1/0/11
Monitoring Alerts: OSPF-Adjacency-EXSTART: Neighbor 8.8.8.8 is stuck. Severity: Major.
Root Cause: The MTU setting on HQ-Edge-01's interface GigabitEthernet1/0/11 is 1500 bytes, whereas the remote peer ISP-Gateway-01 is configured with an MTU of 1470 bytes. OSPF Database Description (DBD) packets are dropped when they exceed the lower MTU limit.
Step-by-Step Troubleshooting:
1. Verify interface MTU settings using 'show interfaces GigabitEthernet1/0/11'.
2. Run 'show ip ospf neighbor' to confirm state.
3. Configure matching MTU values on both ends or set OSPF to ignore MTU mismatches.
Commands Executed:
show ip ospf neighbor
show interfaces GigabitEthernet1/0/11
Actual Output: Local interface MTU: 1500, Peer interface MTU: 1470
Resolution: Adjusted the MTU on HQ-Edge-01's interface GigabitEthernet1/0/11 to 1470 bytes using 'ip mtu 1470' or enabled OSPF MTU ignore using 'ip ospf mtu-ignore'.
Validation Steps: Verify adjacency state reaches FULL by running 'show ip ospf neighbor'.
Rollback Procedure: Restore previous MTU settings: 'interface GigabitEthernet1/0/11' -> 'ip mtu 1500'
Preventive Measures: Ensure WAN interface deployment check-lists specify matching MTU values.
Best Practices: Always keep standard MTU (1500 bytes) across all core interfaces unless Jumbo Frames (9000 bytes) are required.
Security Considerations: Use OSPF MD5 authentication to protect routing adjacency changes.
Automation Opportunity: Ansible playbook implementing interface MTU validations.
AI Troubleshooting Prompt: Identify OSPF adjacency stuck in EXSTART. Align interface MTUs to match 1470 bytes.
Interview Questions: Why does OSPF fail to form adjacency if there is an MTU mismatch? What packet types are affected?
References: Cisco OSPF MTU Mismatch Troubleshooting Guide.

Environment: Enterprise core topology linking SW-Core-A to SW-Distribution-B on VLAN 120.
Business Context: A security policy audit required password rotation on all external BGP connections. After updating SW-Core-A, the BGP peer connection to ISP gateway SW-Distribution-B dropped, breaking internet access for the branch office.
Problem Statement: BGP peering fails to establish due to MD5 authentication password mismatch.
Symptoms: BGP neighbor connection stays in Active or Idle state. No routes are exchanged.
Error Messages: %TCP-6-BADAUTH: No MD5 digest from 10.100.1.2:179 to 10.100.1.1:49152
Logs:
2026-06-27T12:35:01Z SW-Core-A %TCP-6-BADAUTH: Invalid MD5 digest from 10.100.1.2
2026-06-27T12:35:05Z SW-Core-A %BGP-5-ADJCHANGE: neighbor 10.100.1.2 Down: Authentication Failure
Monitoring Alerts: BGP-Neighbor-Auth-Failed: BGP session with 10.100.1.2 is down. Severity: Critical.
Root Cause: The MD5 password configured locally on SW-Core-A was set incorrectly (missing trailing characters) compared to the password on ISP gateway SW-Distribution-B.
Step-by-Step Troubleshooting:
1. Run 'show ip bgp neighbors' to view peering state.
2. Verify the BGP neighbor password command in the active configuration.
3. Verify the password with the ISP administrator.
Commands Executed:
show ip bgp neighbors
show run | section bgp
Actual Output: BGP Neighbor State: Active
Resolution: Corrected the neighbor password configuration on SW-Core-A under BGP AS 66451 to match the peer password.
Validation Steps: Verify session establishes with 'show ip bgp summary'.
Rollback Procedure: Revert to original password string using Cisco commands.
Preventive Measures: Use automated password vault syncing to deploy BGP keys securely and simultaneously.
Best Practices: Rotate passwords only during scheduled maintenance windows with peer operators present.
Security Considerations: Use strong unique passwords for BGP sessions and restrict VTY access.
Automation Opportunity: Ansible task utilizing vault secrets to deploy neighbor passwords.
AI Troubleshooting Prompt: Identify BGP TCP-6-BADAUTH errors. Update the neighbor password config to match the ISP key.
Interview Questions: How does BGP utilize TCP MD5 signatures to protect peering sessions? What port does BGP run on?
References: RFC 2385 - Protection of BGP Sessions via the TCP MD5 Signature Option.

Environment: Enterprise core topology linking WAN-Router-01 to WAN-Router-02 on VLAN 130.
Business Context: An employee connected a personal desktop switch to their cubicle wall jack. Immediately, all network access to that desk was lost, prompting an urgent desktop support ticket.
Problem Statement: Switch access interface shutdown by Spanning Tree BPDU Guard after receiving BPDUs.
Symptoms: Workstation loses link state. Interface LED is solid orange/amber. Link status shows err-disable.
Error Messages: %SPANTREE-2-BLOCK_BPDUGUARD: Received BPDU on port GigabitEthernet1/0/13 with BPDU Guard enabled. Disabling port.
Logs:
2026-06-27T12:40:02Z WAN-Router-01 %PM-4-ERR_DISABLE: bpduguard error detected on GigabitEthernet1/0/13, putting GigabitEthernet1/0/13 in err-disable state
2026-06-27T12:40:03Z WAN-Router-01 %LINK-3-UPDOWN: Interface GigabitEthernet1/0/13, changed state to Down
Monitoring Alerts: Port-Err-Disabled: Interface GigabitEthernet1/0/13 shutdown by BPDU Guard. Severity: Major.
Root Cause: BPDU Guard was active on the interface configured with PortFast. Connecting a switch caused BPDU frames to be sent to WAN-Router-01, which triggered the security block to prevent STP loops.
Step-by-Step Troubleshooting:
1. Run 'show interface GigabitEthernet1/0/13 status' to verify err-disable reason.
2. Unplug the unauthorized switch.
3. Shut down and re-enable the port or configure errdisable recovery.
Commands Executed:
show interface GigabitEthernet1/0/13 status
show spanning-tree summary
Actual Output: Port state: err-disabled
Resolution: Disconnected the rogue switch and executed 'shutdown' / 'no shutdown' on interface GigabitEthernet1/0/13 to clear the err-disable state.
Validation Steps: Confirm client PC receives IP via DHCP and state shows connected.
Rollback Procedure: None required once rogue hardware is removed.
Preventive Measures: Keep BPDU Guard enabled globally on all access ports to block unauthorized switches.
Best Practices: Enable 'errdisable recovery cause bpduguard' to automatically restore ports after a timeout period.
Security Considerations: Use Port Security in conjunction with BPDU Guard to limit MAC address counts.
Automation Opportunity: Automated alert script to notify port location when BPDU Guard triggers.
AI Troubleshooting Prompt: Clear err-disable state on port GigabitEthernet1/0/13 after verifying the unauthorized switch has been disconnected.
Interview Questions: Why is BPDU Guard deployed on ports configured with PortFast? What is the risk if it is disabled?
References: Cisco Spanning Tree BPDU Guard Configuration Guide.

Environment: Enterprise core topology linking vEdge-Branch-1 to vSmart-Control-1 on VLAN 140.
Business Context: To increase database synchronization speeds between vEdge-Branch-1 and vSmart-Control-1, engineers bundled two interfaces into a Port-channel. However, the throughput dropped below single link speeds.
Problem Statement: LACP Port-channel bundle degraded due to port speed/duplex mismatch on member interfaces.
Symptoms: Port-channel throughput is low. One interface shows suspended. Interface packet drops increase.
Error Messages: %EC-5-CANNOT_BUNDLE2: GigabitEthernet1/0/2 is not compatible with GigabitEthernet1/0/1 and cannot be bundled
Logs:
2026-06-27T12:45:01Z vEdge-Branch-1 %EC-5-CANNOT_BUNDLE2: Gi1/0/2 speed is 100Mb, Gi1/0/1 speed is 1Gb
2026-06-27T12:45:04Z vEdge-Branch-1 %EC-5-PORT_SUSPENDED: Interface Gi1/0/2 suspended in Port-channel 6
Monitoring Alerts: LACP-Bundle-Degraded: Port-channel 6 has suspended members. Severity: Warning.
Root Cause: Interface GigabitEthernet1/0/2 negotiated a speed of 100Mbps (half-duplex) due to a bad patch cable, while GigabitEthernet1/0/1 negotiated 1Gbps. LACP requires all member ports to have identical speed, duplex, and VLAN settings.
Step-by-Step Troubleshooting:
1. Run 'show etherchannel summary' to find suspended ports.
2. Check physical link speeds using 'show interfaces status'.
3. Replace the bad cable or force speed settings.
Commands Executed:
show etherchannel summary
show interfaces status
Expected Output: All ports bundled (P state) and running at 1Gbps.
Actual Output: Gi1/0/2 marked as (s) suspended due to speed mismatch.
Resolution: Replaced the degraded network cable on Gi1/0/2, allowing it to negotiate 1Gbps, resolving the bundle conflict.
Validation Steps: Verify bundle status shows 'P' (in Port-channel) for all members.
Rollback Procedure: None needed.
Preventive Measures: Enforce auto-negotiation on all bundle members and test cabling profiles.
Best Practices: Ensure bundle member ports are spread across different switch linecards for hardware redundancy.
Security Considerations: Use LACP system priorities to prevent spoofed switches from hijacking the bundle.
Automation Opportunity: Python script checking port-channel member counts via SNMP.
AI Troubleshooting Prompt: Identify suspended ports in Port-channel 6. Replace degraded links and verify state.
Interview Questions: What parameters must match for physical interfaces to be successfully bundled into an LACP group?
References: IEEE 802.3ad - Link Aggregation Control Protocol.

Environment: Enterprise core topology linking WAN-Router-01 to WAN-Router-02 on VLAN 10.
Business Context: A bandwidth upgrade on the WAN link between WAN-Router-01 and WAN-Router-02 changed the physical interface from Ethernet to Fiber. The link was established, but routing tables failed to sync, stopping inter-office email access.
Problem Statement: OSPF neighbor adjacency stuck in EXSTART/EXCHANGE state due to MTU mismatch on peer interfaces.
Symptoms: OSPF adjacency fails to reach the FULL state, cycling between EXSTART and EXCHANGE. Routes are not advertised.
Error Messages: %OSPF-5-ADJCHG: Process 1, Nbr 192.168.99.254 on GigabitEthernet1/0/21 from LOADING to DOWN, Neighbor Down: Interface MTU mismatch
Logs:
2026-06-27T12:30:00Z WAN-Router-01 %OSPF-5-ADJCHG: Neighbor 192.168.99.254 stuck in EXSTART
2026-06-27T12:30:04Z WAN-Router-01 OSPF: Retransmitting DBD packet to 192.168.99.254 on GigabitEthernet1/0/21
Monitoring Alerts: OSPF-Adjacency-EXSTART: Neighbor 192.168.99.254 is stuck. Severity: Major.
Root Cause: The MTU setting on WAN-Router-01's interface GigabitEthernet1/0/21 is 1500 bytes, whereas the remote peer WAN-Router-02 is configured with an MTU of 1490 bytes. OSPF Database Description (DBD) packets are dropped when they exceed the lower MTU limit.
Step-by-Step Troubleshooting:
1. Verify interface MTU settings using 'show interfaces GigabitEthernet1/0/21'.
2. Run 'show ip ospf neighbor' to confirm state.
3. Configure matching MTU values on both ends or set OSPF to ignore MTU mismatches.
Commands Executed:
show ip ospf neighbor
show interfaces GigabitEthernet1/0/21
Actual Output: Local interface MTU: 1500, Peer interface MTU: 1490
Resolution: Adjusted the MTU on WAN-Router-01's interface GigabitEthernet1/0/21 to 1490 bytes using 'ip mtu 1490' or enabled OSPF MTU ignore using 'ip ospf mtu-ignore'.
Validation Steps: Verify adjacency state reaches FULL by running 'show ip ospf neighbor'.
Rollback Procedure: Restore previous MTU settings: 'interface GigabitEthernet1/0/21' -> 'ip mtu 1500'
Preventive Measures: Ensure WAN interface deployment check-lists specify matching MTU values.
Best Practices: Always keep standard MTU (1500 bytes) across all core interfaces unless Jumbo Frames (9000 bytes) are required.
Security Considerations: Use OSPF MD5 authentication to protect routing adjacency changes.
Automation Opportunity: Ansible playbook implementing interface MTU validations.
AI Troubleshooting Prompt: Identify OSPF adjacency stuck in EXSTART. Align interface MTUs to match 1490 bytes.
Interview Questions: Why does OSPF fail to form adjacency if there is an MTU mismatch? What packet types are affected?
References: Cisco OSPF MTU Mismatch Troubleshooting Guide.

Environment: Enterprise core topology linking vEdge-Branch-1 to vSmart-Control-1 on VLAN 20.
Business Context: A security policy audit required password rotation on all external BGP connections. After updating vEdge-Branch-1, the BGP peer connection to ISP gateway vSmart-Control-1 dropped, breaking internet access for the branch office.
Problem Statement: BGP peering fails to establish due to MD5 authentication password mismatch.
Symptoms: BGP neighbor connection stays in Active or Idle state. No routes are exchanged.
Logs:
2026-06-27T12:35:01Z vEdge-Branch-1 %TCP-6-BADAUTH: Invalid MD5 digest from 198.51.100.10
2026-06-27T12:35:05Z vEdge-Branch-1 %BGP-5-ADJCHANGE: neighbor 198.51.100.10 Down: Authentication Failure
Monitoring Alerts: BGP-Neighbor-Auth-Failed: BGP session with 198.51.100.10 is down. Severity: Critical.
Root Cause: The MD5 password configured locally on vEdge-Branch-1 was set incorrectly (missing trailing characters) compared to the password on ISP gateway vSmart-Control-1.
Step-by-Step Troubleshooting:
1. Run 'show ip bgp neighbors' to view peering state.
2. Verify the BGP neighbor password command in the active configuration.
3. Verify the password with the ISP administrator.
Commands Executed:
show ip bgp neighbors
show run | section bgp
Actual Output: BGP Neighbor State: Active
Resolution: Corrected the neighbor password configuration on vEdge-Branch-1 under BGP AS 66461 to match the peer password.
Validation Steps: Verify session establishes with 'show ip bgp summary'.
Rollback Procedure: Revert to original password string using Cisco commands.
Preventive Measures: Use automated password vault syncing to deploy BGP keys securely and simultaneously.
Best Practices: Rotate passwords only during scheduled maintenance windows with peer operators present.
Security Considerations: Use strong unique passwords for BGP sessions and restrict VTY access.
Automation Opportunity: Ansible task utilizing vault secrets to deploy neighbor passwords.
AI Troubleshooting Prompt: Identify BGP TCP-6-BADAUTH errors. Update the neighbor password config to match the ISP key.
Interview Questions: How does BGP utilize TCP MD5 signatures to protect peering sessions? What port does BGP run on?
References: RFC 2385 - Protection of BGP Sessions via the TCP MD5 Signature Option.

Environment: Enterprise core topology linking PE-Router-01 to P-Router-02 on VLAN 30.
Business Context: An employee connected a personal desktop switch to their cubicle wall jack. Immediately, all network access to that desk was lost, prompting an urgent desktop support ticket.
Problem Statement: Switch access interface shutdown by Spanning Tree BPDU Guard after receiving BPDUs.
Symptoms: Workstation loses link state. Interface LED is solid orange/amber. Link status shows err-disable.
Error Messages: %SPANTREE-2-BLOCK_BPDUGUARD: Received BPDU on port GigabitEthernet1/0/23 with BPDU Guard enabled. Disabling port.
Logs:
2026-06-27T12:40:02Z PE-Router-01 %PM-4-ERR_DISABLE: bpduguard error detected on GigabitEthernet1/0/23, putting GigabitEthernet1/0/23 in err-disable state
2026-06-27T12:40:03Z PE-Router-01 %LINK-3-UPDOWN: Interface GigabitEthernet1/0/23, changed state to Down
Monitoring Alerts: Port-Err-Disabled: Interface GigabitEthernet1/0/23 shutdown by BPDU Guard. Severity: Major.
Root Cause: BPDU Guard was active on the interface configured with PortFast. Connecting a switch caused BPDU frames to be sent to PE-Router-01, which triggered the security block to prevent STP loops.
Step-by-Step Troubleshooting:
1. Run 'show interface GigabitEthernet1/0/23 status' to verify err-disable reason.
2. Unplug the unauthorized switch.
3. Shut down and re-enable the port or configure errdisable recovery.
Commands Executed:
show interface GigabitEthernet1/0/23 status
show spanning-tree summary
Actual Output: Port state: err-disabled
Resolution: Disconnected the rogue switch and executed 'shutdown' / 'no shutdown' on interface GigabitEthernet1/0/23 to clear the err-disable state.
Validation Steps: Confirm client PC receives IP via DHCP and state shows connected.
Rollback Procedure: None required once rogue hardware is removed.
Preventive Measures: Keep BPDU Guard enabled globally on all access ports to block unauthorized switches.
Best Practices: Enable 'errdisable recovery cause bpduguard' to automatically restore ports after a timeout period.
Security Considerations: Use Port Security in conjunction with BPDU Guard to limit MAC address counts.
Automation Opportunity: Automated alert script to notify port location when BPDU Guard triggers.
AI Troubleshooting Prompt: Clear err-disable state on port GigabitEthernet1/0/23 after verifying the unauthorized switch has been disconnected.
Interview Questions: Why is BPDU Guard deployed on ports configured with PortFast? What is the risk if it is disabled?
References: Cisco Spanning Tree BPDU Guard Configuration Guide.

Environment: Enterprise core topology linking ASA-Firewall-01 to ASA-Firewall-02 on VLAN 40.
Business Context: To increase database synchronization speeds between ASA-Firewall-01 and ASA-Firewall-02, engineers bundled two interfaces into a Port-channel. However, the throughput dropped below single link speeds.
Problem Statement: LACP Port-channel bundle degraded due to port speed/duplex mismatch on member interfaces.
Symptoms: Port-channel throughput is low. One interface shows suspended. Interface packet drops increase.
Error Messages: %EC-5-CANNOT_BUNDLE2: GigabitEthernet1/0/2 is not compatible with GigabitEthernet1/0/1 and cannot be bundled
Logs:
2026-06-27T12:45:01Z ASA-Firewall-01 %EC-5-CANNOT_BUNDLE2: Gi1/0/2 speed is 100Mb, Gi1/0/1 speed is 1Gb
2026-06-27T12:45:04Z ASA-Firewall-01 %EC-5-PORT_SUSPENDED: Interface Gi1/0/2 suspended in Port-channel 8
Monitoring Alerts: LACP-Bundle-Degraded: Port-channel 8 has suspended members. Severity: Warning.
Root Cause: Interface GigabitEthernet1/0/2 negotiated a speed of 100Mbps (half-duplex) due to a bad patch cable, while GigabitEthernet1/0/1 negotiated 1Gbps. LACP requires all member ports to have identical speed, duplex, and VLAN settings.
Step-by-Step Troubleshooting:
1. Run 'show etherchannel summary' to find suspended ports.
2. Check physical link speeds using 'show interfaces status'.
3. Replace the bad cable or force speed settings.
Commands Executed:
show etherchannel summary
show interfaces status
Expected Output: All ports bundled (P state) and running at 1Gbps.
Actual Output: Gi1/0/2 marked as (s) suspended due to speed mismatch.
Resolution: Replaced the degraded network cable on Gi1/0/2, allowing it to negotiate 1Gbps, resolving the bundle conflict.
Validation Steps: Verify bundle status shows 'P' (in Port-channel) for all members.
Rollback Procedure: None needed.
Preventive Measures: Enforce auto-negotiation on all bundle members and test cabling profiles.
Best Practices: Ensure bundle member ports are spread across different switch linecards for hardware redundancy.
Security Considerations: Use LACP system priorities to prevent spoofed switches from hijacking the bundle.
Automation Opportunity: Python script checking port-channel member counts via SNMP.
AI Troubleshooting Prompt: Identify suspended ports in Port-channel 8. Replace degraded links and verify state.
Interview Questions: What parameters must match for physical interfaces to be successfully bundled into an LACP group?
References: IEEE 802.3ad - Link Aggregation Control Protocol.

Environment: Enterprise core topology linking HQ-Core-Router-01 to Branch-Router-03 on VLAN 50.
Business Context: Users at a branch office report they can browse simple web sites, but cannot download large files or open core database pages over the GRE tunnel linked to HQ-Core-Router-01.
Problem Statement: GRE Tunnel drops large packets due to PMTUD path failure (DF bit set).
Symptoms: Web sites hang indefinitely during load. Ping with small packet size (1000 bytes) works, but ping with 1500 bytes and DF bit set fails.
Error Messages: %TUN-5-MTU_ERR: Packet dropped due to GRE encapsulation size exceeding interface MTU
Logs:
2026-06-27T12:50:01Z HQ-Core-Router-01 %IP-4-FRAG_REQUIRED: Packet from 10.10.100.5 to 192.168.10.10 requires fragmentation but DF bit set
2026-06-27T12:50:04Z HQ-Core-Router-01 ICMP: sending dst unreachable; frag needed to 10.10.100.5
Monitoring Alerts: GRE-Tunnel-Frag-Error: Packet drop rate on Tunnel0 exceeds threshold. Severity: Major.
Root Cause: The GRE tunnel adds a 24-byte header, reducing the payload MTU to 1476. The client PC sends 1500-byte packets with the Don't Fragment (DF) bit set. Upstream devices drop these packets without sending ICMP Type 3 Code 4 (frag needed) because ICMP was blocked by firewalls.
Step-by-Step Troubleshooting:
1. Run ping tests over the tunnel with varying packet sizes and DF bit enabled.
2. Verify TCP MSS adjustment configurations on the tunnel interfaces.
3. Configure 'ip tcp adjust-mss 1360' on the tunnel interfaces.
Commands Executed:
ping 192.168.10.10 size 1450 df-bit
show interface tunnel 0
Expected Output: Successful ping at 1450 bytes with DF bit.
Actual Output: Ping fails for packets larger than 1420 bytes.
Resolution: Configured 'ip tcp adjust-mss 1360' and 'ip mtu 1400' on the Tunnel0 interface of HQ-Core-Router-01 and the branch router.
Validation Steps: Verify client web page loads fully and runs without packet dropouts.
Rollback Procedure:
no ip tcp adjust-mss
no ip mtu
Preventive Measures: Configure TCP MSS adjustment on all GRE/IPSec tunnels during deployment.
Best Practices: Set TCP MSS to 1360 bytes on VPN interfaces to account for IPSec and GRE encapsulation overhead.
Security Considerations: Permit ICMP Type 3 Code 4 in edge firewall rules to support Path MTU Discovery.
Automation Opportunity: Ansible playbook implementing interface MTU and MSS settings on tunnel interfaces.
AI Troubleshooting Prompt: Resolve GRE packet fragmentation drops. Set tunnel interface MTU to 1400 and TCP MSS to 1360.
Interview Questions: Why does GRE encapsulation require adjustments to the TCP Maximum Segment Size (MSS)?
References: RFC 2923 - TCP Problems with Path MTU Discovery.
